skill.name: generate-assets
skill.description: Generate web-ready image assets with Replicate FLUX 1.1 Pro, optional background removal, sizing, and WebP optimization.
skill.summary: Creates optimized website image assets using Replicate image models and a local TypeScript helper script.
skill.author: AayushBaniya2006

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer correctly identified script execution, network use, filesystem writes, and environment variable access, but many Ruby backtick and weak crypto alerts are false positives from Markdown, strings, and template literals. No malicious intent or prompt injection was found, but the script has an unsafe user-controlled output path that can write outside the intended asset directory.

high_findings.title: User-Controlled Output Path Can Escape Asset Directory
high_findings.description: The script accepts folder and filename arguments, joins them into target paths, creates directories, and writes the optimized image without validating that the final path remains under public/assets/images. A crafted folder such as a parent-directory traversal could cause writes outside the intended asset area.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: There is a direct flow from process.argv values into path.join and fs.writeFileSync. I found no normalization, allowlist, or boundary check before directory creation and file writing.

medium_findings.title: Credentialed Replicate Network Calls and Remote Downloads
medium_findings.description: The script reads REPLICATE_API_TOKEN, creates Replicate predictions, and downloads image outputs from returned URLs. This is expected for the skill, but it sends user prompts and generated asset data to a third-party service.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network and token use are explicit and semantically required by the asset generator. I found no evidence that the token is sent anywhere except the Replicate client.

medium_findings.title: Bash-Based Helper Script Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill declares Bash access and instructs users to run a local TypeScript helper through pnpm. This is normal for a code-generation utility, but it increases execution risk because command arguments come from the task context.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill explicitly requires Bash and provides a pnpm command. I did not find child_process usage inside the script, so the risk is from intentional tool execution rather than hidden command execution.

medium_findings.title: Background Removal Disables Content Moderation
medium_findings.description: The background removal request sets content_moderation to false. This may be intentional for asset processing, but it weakens provider-side moderation during that step.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The setting is explicit in the Replicate background removal input. The security impact depends on deployment policy, but the code clearly disables that moderation control.

low_findings.title: Static Ruby Backtick and Weak Crypto Alerts Are Mostly False Positives
low_findings.description: Many reported Ruby backtick, weak crypto, and reconnaissance findings are caused by Markdown inline code, aspect ratio strings, model names, template literals, or style text. I found no Ruby execution, cryptographic operation, or system reconnaissance behavior in those cited contexts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are documentation values or string arrays, not executable Ruby or crypto APIs. This strongly supports dismissing those specific static findings.

low_findings.title: Base64 Data URL Decoding Is Expected but Unbounded
low_findings.description: The script decodes data URL image outputs into buffers. This is not obfuscation in context, but there is no visible size limit before Buffer allocation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The base64 handling is part of normal image download processing. The residual concern is resource exhaustion, not hidden code or credential exfiltration.

dangerous_patterns.title: Path Join With User-Controlled Folder Followed by File Write
dangerous_patterns.description: The output folder and filename are derived from command arguments and used in mkdirSync and writeFileSync. The final path is not checked against an allowed base directory.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The unsafe pattern is direct and easy to trace from argument parsing to filesystem write. No path containment guard is present in the inspected lines.

dangerous_patterns.title: Third-Party API Calls With Local Environment Token
dangerous_patterns.description: The script initializes Replicate with REPLICATE_API_TOKEN and creates model predictions. This is legitimate for the feature, but it exposes prompts and generated asset workflow data to the external provider.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Credentialed network use is explicit and tied to Replicate SDK calls. I did not find evidence of unrelated domains or token exfiltration.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote URL Fetch Into Image Processing Pipeline
dangerous_patterns.description: The script fetches URLs returned by the image provider and passes the downloaded bytes into sharp. Provider output should still be treated as untrusted remote content.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The fetch-to-buffer-to-sharp flow is clear. The source is expected Replicate output, but the script does not add size, MIME type, or domain validation.

content.value_statement: Website teams need polished image assets that match component size and format needs. This skill generates Replicate images, optionally removes backgrounds, and saves optimized WebP files.
actual_capabilities: Generates images with the Replicate FLUX 1.1 Pro model.
Enhances prompts with UI, layout, theme, and quality details.
Chooses aspect ratios for hero, feature, transformation, foreground, and general assets.
Detects existing image dimensions before replacing an asset.
Can call a background removal model for transparent foreground assets.
Optimizes generated images as WebP files with sharp.
limitations: Requires a valid REPLICATE_API_TOKEN in the local environment.
Sends prompts and generated image workflow data to Replicate.
Does not validate output paths against the intended asset directory.
Requires project dependencies such as replicate and sharp to be installed.

use_cases.title: Create Hero Artwork
use_cases.description: Generate wide WebP images for landing page heroes while matching existing dimensions when replacing files.

use_cases.title: Produce Feature Visuals
use_cases.description: Create square or four-by-three visuals that show product capabilities, dashboards, or interface states.

use_cases.title: Build Transparent Foreground Assets
use_cases.description: Generate decorative elements, remove backgrounds, and save transparent assets for web layouts.

prompt_templates.title: Generate a Basic Hero Image
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a new page hero.
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a hero asset for a SaaS analytics dashboard. Use a 16:9 aspect ratio, filename analytics-hero, folder hero, and no transparent background.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Feature Card Asset
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request for a reusable feature visual.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a feature showcase image for AI content review. Use a 1:1 aspect ratio, filename ai-review-feature, folder features, and a web product interface style.

prompt_templates.title: Replace an Existing Image
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request where current asset dimensions should be preserved.
prompt_templates.prompt: Replace the existing onboarding transformation image. Match the current dimensions, use filename onboarding-flow, folder transformations, and show a before-and-after workflow.

prompt_templates.title: Generate a Transparent Foreground Element
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request for composable decorative assets.
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a transparent foreground asset of subtle interface particles. Use filename ui-particles, folder foreground, asset type foreground, and transparent background enabled.

output_examples.input: Request a 16:9 hero image for a modern dashboard.
output_examples.output: A generated WebP hero image saved in the hero asset folder, with dimensions suitable for a wide landing page section.

output_examples.input: Request a foreground decorative asset with transparency.
output_examples.output: A transparent WebP foreground asset after background removal, ready to layer into a component.

output_examples.input: Request replacement of an existing feature image.
output_examples.output: A new optimized WebP image that uses the detected dimensions of the existing file.

best_practices: Use clear prompts that name the subject, interface context, and visual purpose.
Keep generated files inside public/assets/images until path validation is added.
Review generated images before committing them to production branches.
anti_patterns: Do not include secrets, private customer data, or unreleased product details in prompts.
Do not pass parent-directory paths or absolute paths as folder or filename values.
Do not rely on generated assets without checking brand fit, accessibility, and file size.

faq.question: What service does this skill use for image generation?
faq.answer: It uses Replicate with the FLUX 1.1 Pro model for image generation.

faq.question: Does it need an API key?
faq.answer: Yes. It requires REPLICATE_API_TOKEN in the environment before the script can run.

faq.question: Where are generated assets saved?
faq.answer: The intended location is public/assets/images, with optional subfolders for asset types.

faq.question: Can it create transparent images?
faq.answer: Yes. It can call a background removal model for foreground or transparent assets.

faq.question: Why is the security risk rated high?
faq.answer: The script writes files using user-controlled folder and filename values without a path boundary check.

faq.question: Is there evidence of malicious behavior?
faq.answer: No evidence of malicious intent, prompt injection, or credential exfiltration was found during review.
